exemplar.title: In what ways is the duality of human nature achieved through Gothic elements in Truman Capoteâs âIn Cold Bloodâ and Patricia Highsmithâs âStrangers on a Trainâ?

subject.title: English A Lang & Lit
subject.description:
**IB English A Language & Literature** is a **Group 1** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **the study of language and literature through a wide variety of texts and media from different periods, styles, and cultures**, with a strong focus on **critical analysis of both literary and non-literary texts, examining how language choices create meaning and effect in different contexts**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper 1** | Text analysis | 1h 15m | 2h 15m | 35% (SL), 25% (HL) |
| **Paper 2** | Comparative essay | 1h 45m | 1h 45m | 35% (SL), 25% (HL)  |
| **HL Essay** | Written assignment | N/A | 1200-1500 words | 25% (HL only) |
| **Individual Oral** | Oral presentation | 15 minutes | 15 minutes | 30% (SL), 20% (HL) |

strengths: Research question is clearly stated, focused and effectively connected to the analysis throughout the essay
Topic is communicated accurately with clear purpose and consistent signposting in section headings
weaknesses: Methodology relies on a limited range of non-scholarly web sources without sufficient justification
Some digressive asides (e.g. colour-theory aside) momentarily distract from the central focus
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two of the three Focus and Method strands (question focus and topic clarity) fully meet the 5â6 band, but the methodology strand exhibits shortcomings in source selection and scholarly justification.  The overall performance is therefore solidly within the top band but not flawless, warranting 5 of 6.

strengths: Demonstrates good knowledge of primary texts with coherent integration of Jungian theoretical concepts
Terminology such as âdoppelgÃ¤nger,â âshadow,â and âGothic motifâ is generally accurate and well applied
weaknesses: Supporting scholarship is uneven, with over-reliance on journalistic or popular-press sources
Occasional generic language and lack of precise Gothic terminology weaken the depth of subject-specific understanding
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student shows solid familiarity with the texts and uses appropriate terminology, placing performance in the 3â4 band.  Uneven scholarly support and some generic language prevent movement into the top band, so a score of 4 of 6 is appropriate.

strengths: Sustained close reading with well-supported interpretation linking narrative details to Jungian theory
Effective cross-textual synthesis demonstrating nuanced critical thinking
weaknesses: Sections occasionally lapse into plot summary without sufficient analytical development
Limited engagement with counter-arguments or broader implications in the conclusion
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two of the three Critical Thinking strands sit firmly in the 7â9 band, while the evaluation strand shows adequate but not fully critical engagement (4â6 band).  Overall performance aligns with the mid-band 7â9 descriptor, so 8 of 12 is justified.

strengths: Clear and appropriate overall structure with title page, table of contents and separate primary/secondary bibliography
Section headings guide the reader effectively through the argument
weaknesses: Inconsistent citation styles (mix of superscripts and incomplete footnotes)
Formatting irregularities in the table of contents and some overly long paragraphs reduce professional polish
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All required elements of Presentation are present, placing the work in the 3â4 band, but persistent minor formatting lapses prevent a full-mark award.  A score of 3 of 4 reflects solid execution with room for improvement.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The student outlines a sound comparativeâclose-reading method and supplements this with secondary criticism and Jungian theory, showing basic methodological awareness.  The annotated reading of two primary texts is appropriate and the bibliography distinguishes primary and secondary works.  

However, the range of secondary sources is modest and contains several popular-culture or web articles (Medium, Dunn-Edwards Paints, Smashing Magazine) that are not scholarly.  No clear rationale is offered for their inclusion, and source limitations are acknowledged only briefly in the conclusion.  

Because the methodology is âmostly completeâ and âgenerally relevantâ but lacks consistent evidence of effective, scholarly selection, the performance matches the 3â4 band.

The essay contains a correctly formatted title page, table of contents, page numbers and a bibliography separated into primary/secondary sources.  Headings are clear, and the overall visual presentation is neat and readable.  

Citation style is applied inconsistently (superscript numbers mixed with incomplete footnote details; some URLs truncated), and a few long paragraphs could be broken up for better readability.  These issues marginally detract from professional polish.  

Because layout requirements are met but minor formatting lapses persist, the work meets the criteria for the 3â4 band; a score of 3 recognises solid but not flawless execution.

Primary researchâthe close reading of two substantial textsâis thorough, and most secondary materials do relate to the research focus (Jungian essays, colour theory, articles on Capote).  The student shows discernment in selecting theoretical frames (Jung, Arendt) that illuminate duality.  

However, the scholarly depth of the secondary list is restricted; several sources lack academic rigour, and some references are thinly integrated.  These limitations prevent the research base from being described as consistently âexcellent.â  

Still, the breadth and relevance to the question are clear, fulfilling the âgood â¦ mostly relevantâ description of the 7â9 band; a mid-band score of 8 is appropriate.

The research question is placed prominently in the title and reiterated in the introduction and conclusion, allowing the reader to understand precisely what is being investigated. Headings such as âSimilar Gothic Characteristicsâ and âShadow and Egoâ keep the commentary tightly anchored to the stated focus on duality and Gothic elements. 

Throughout the body the student consistently signposts how each Gothic motif links back to duality (e.g., âBy making the audience like Smith â¦ emphasises the duality of his characterâ).  Although a few digressive sentences appear (brief colour-theory aside, biographical comments), they do not obscure the overall clarity of purpose. 

Because the topic is communicated accurately, with a clear sense of direction and minimal redundancy, the strand aligns with the highest rubric band (5â6).

The student constructs a coherent comparative argument, moving thematically through shared and divergent Gothic elements and culminating in a clear overall judgement.  There is some attempt to weigh ambiguities (e.g., Capoteâs bias, Brunoâs motives) and to suggest further research.  

Yet evaluative engagement with counter-arguments or source limitations is brief, and the conclusion largely restates findings without probing their implications.  This indicates âadequate argument â¦ with some inconsistencies and basic evaluationâ rather than sustained critical appraisal.  

Accordingly the work earns a solid position in the 4â6 band, meriting a score of 6.

The essay offers sustained close analysis of narrative detailâe.g., Smithâs dream symbolism, spatial isolation, the murder sceneâs pacingâand links these to Jungian theory with generally convincing logic.  Claims are normally supported by quotation or paraphrase.  

Some sections lapse into plot summary, and a few assertions regarding reader response or colour symbolism are not fully evidenced.  Even so, the discussion usually advances beyond description to interpretive commentary, and cross-textual synthesis (e.g., equating Bruno with Hainesâs shadow) demonstrates analytical ambition.  

Given the mostly well-supported reasoning with occasional gaps, performance fits the 7â9 band; a score of 8 recognises good but not uniformly excellent analytical depth.

The EE follows IB structural conventions: title page with word count, clear table of contents, logical section headings, introduction-body-conclusion sequence and separate bibliography.  Transitions between thematic sections are generally smooth, enabling coherent progression.  

Minor proportional imbalances (some very short subsections) and occasional redundancy do not undermine overall appropriateness.  All required structural elements are present, warranting placement in the top band (3â4).  A full score of 4 reflects the comprehensive fulfilment of structural expectations.

Frequent quotation and paraphrase from both Capote and Highsmith demonstrate solid familiarity with plot, character and Gothic convention.  The student weaves in Jungian terminology and contextual detail (e.g., Capoteâs relationship with Smith) to frame interpretations, reflecting good subject knowledge.  

Nevertheless, the supporting scholarship is uneven: several sources are journalistic or general-interest rather than peer-reviewed, and the argument occasionally relies on assertion where critical evidence would be expected.  These factors limit the effectiveness of source use.  

Overall, knowledge is âgoodâ and coherently conveyed, yet falls short of the sustained expertise and critical range required for the top band, placing it in 3â4 with a score of 4.

The essay employs literary and psychological vocabularyââdoppelgÃ¤nger,â âbanality of evil,â âshadow,â âego,â âGothic motif,â âomniscient narrationââaccurately and in context, evidencing firm control of key concepts.  The terminology is integrated smoothly into analysis rather than appended artificially.  

While minor slips appear (e.g., âsymbolisticâ), these do not impede understanding and remain infrequent.  The majority of specialised terms are applied correctly and consistently to advance interpretation.  

Given the largely precise and sustained use of subject-specific language, this aligns with the 5â6 band; a score of 5 recognises slight lapses without lowering the overall standard.

The research question is concise, comparative and solidly literary: âIn what ways is the duality of human nature achieved through Gothic elementsâ¦?â  It is narrow enough to be explored within 4,000 words and is referenced explicitly at key junctures (introduction, transitions, conclusion).  

Links between the question and the ensuing analysis are explicit; every major section names a Gothic device and ties it back to duality.  There is no evidence of drift into unrelated thematic territory.  

Given its clarity, manageability and constant integration into discussion, the question meets the descriptor of being âclearly stated, focused and effectively connected,â warranting the top band (5â6).

annotations.comment: The discussion of Miriamâs murder delivers incisive interpretative insight into character psychology and moral ambiguity, advancing the central argument.
The bibliography is clearly divided into primary and secondary documents, meeting IB expectations for source categorization.
Long URLs are split across lines and include extraneous fragments; use a consistent, truncated URL format or DOI for clarity.
The comparative empathy analysis is insightful but would benefit from reflecting on possible counter-readings or limitations of this interpretation.
The symbolic reading of Smithâs dream richly applies Jungian motifs, demonstrating strong source knowledge and conceptual integration.
The subsection on curiosity ends abruptly and lacks full thematic development; expanding analysis to link curiosity more directly to Gothic duality will enhance depth.
Descriptions of isolation are accurate but use generic language; integrating precise Gothic terminology (e.g., uncanny, liminality) would demonstrate stronger subject knowledge.
The word count is provided at the start and falls within the IB limit, satisfying the formal formatting requirement.
The title clearly states the research question, fulfilling the requirement to define focus upfront and guiding reader expectations effectively.
The TOC entries use uneven leader dots and alignments; consistent formatting would improve professional presentation.
The introduction communicates the research question clearly and establishes its relevance, anchoring the discussion to a focused literary inquiry.
The table of contents is clearly structured with all major sections listed, enhancing navigability.
The methodology description lists secondary web articles without justifying their scholarly relevance; a rationale for source selection would strengthen methodological rigor.
A reliance on a Medium article for historical context suggests a limited methodological range; seek authoritative architectural or sociological studies on rural isolation.
The psychological analysis of Hainesâs emotional descent is compelling and connects narrative detail to reader empathy, illustrating interpretive sophistication.
Section headings effectively segment thematic analysis, guiding the reader through comparative Gothic characteristics.
Analysis here relies heavily on narrative summary; deeper engagement with interpretive nuance and critical evidence would yield a more persuasive argument.
Including popular sources (Medium, Dunn-Edwards) without scholarly justification weakens the source selection; consider prioritizing peer-reviewed criticism.
Footnote style shifts between full citation and âibidâ; adopt one consistent system to enhance readability and professional polish.
A paragraph break is missing before a new thematic shift, resulting in a run-on block; separating ideas will improve reader comprehension.
Good use of Holcomb quotes, but the link to Gothic atmosphere needs elaboration; explain how decay imagery attends to literary conventions of the genre.
This concluding thought on subconscious conflict would benefit from direct textual evidence or quotation to ground the claim in the primary text.
This short standalone sentence interrupts the flow; integrate it into surrounding analysis or expand to fully develop its point.
Page numbering and footnote placement are consistent, but consider consolidating author-date in-text citations to reduce reliance on endnotes.
The analysis around Strangers on a Train could explicitly tie each example back to the research question to maintain focus and coherence.
Very long opening sentence here; consider dividing into two sentences to maintain clarity and readability.
Applying a paint-manufacturer blog to colour symbolism undermines academic rigor; replace with peer-reviewed scholarship on colour theory.
Introducing Jungâs shadow and ego concepts structures the theoretical framework well, anchoring the comparative analysis in established psychology.
The cross-textual synthesis of shadow in Highsmithâs characters versus Capoteâs is sophisticated, illustrating nuanced critical thinking.
The conclusion effectively summarizes findings but stops short of evaluating broader implications or suggesting concrete avenues for future research.
Secondary sources are presented in a raw list without consistent numbering or indentation; standardize to MLA or APA style for professionalism.
